title: The Old Camp-Oven
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We don't keep a grand piano in our hut beside the creek,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½m pretty certain Hannah couldnן¿½t bang it, anyhow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But weן¿½ve got one box of music, and Iן¿½d rather hear its squeak
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than the daisiest cantata thatן¿½s been fashioned up to now.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s an old camp-oven merely, with a handle made of wire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But no organ built could nearly compensate to me for it
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I come off graft and find it playing tunes before the fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½m feeling sort of vacant, but just wonder fully fit.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In its sizzle, sizzle, sizzle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a thousand little airs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And no man can sit and grizzle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Bout his troubles and his cares
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the flames are gaily winding,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the tea is down to brew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the old camp-ovenן¿½s grinding
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the reels he ever knew.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the wet winds meet and whip me in the early winter nights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the hissing hailstones clip me all the way across the flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As I battle forן¿½ards, water-logged, toward the beckoning lights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is always there a welcome to console a chap for that.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For my little wife is beaming brisk and bright beside the lamp,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the old camp-ovenן¿½s going. Gosh! I feel just like a kid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As I peel and sluice so slippy, and I hear the storm winds vamp
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the singing of the oven when the missus lifts the lid.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a sizzle and a splutter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a whirr of many harps;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½s the instrument can utter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such a maze of flats and sharps?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not for me the great creations
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the old camp-oven plays
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Home Sweet Home,ן¿½ with variations,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the end of working days.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the evenings dim and hazy, stretched outside along a butt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feeling reasonably lazy, blowing clouds that curl and climb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I can hear the old camp-oven on the logs before the hut
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ripping out a mellow chorus that just suits the place and time.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If we strike it in the ranges, or The Windmill turns out well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I suppose thereן¿½ll be some changes, and Iן¿½ll want to make things gee;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the time will never happen when Iן¿½ll be so steep a swell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That the old camp-ovenן¿½s measure wonן¿½t be melody to me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Neath its bubble, bubble, bubble,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s the lilt of jigs and reels;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the common kind of trouble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That the horney-handed feels
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is wiped out in half a minute
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the restfulness it brings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the peaceful rapture in it
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the old camp-oven sings
meaning:
This evocative piece by Edward Dyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Edward Dyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
